Below the description you'll see the four feedback categories (results, economy, identity, ranking) with a bar per category, showing how strongly that member's archetype weighs each one. This is the same weighting the board engine uses to pick which member 'speaks' about a given target reaction, now made visible instead of only used internally.

boardTradeoffWarning.q: What is the tradeoff warning on a plan card?
boardTradeoffWarning.a: Some board requests you approve (e.g. easing results pressure or loosening identity requirements) come with a locked-in cost for your NEXT plan negotiation, e.g. one extra identity rider requirement or a higher sponsor growth target. When that's the case, the plan card shows a small tradeoff card explaining exactly what's coming, so you're not surprised at the next negotiation.

boardPassiveModifierLayer.q: What is the small line about the sponsor modifier under my satisfaction number?
boardPassiveModifierLayer.a: This is layer 1 of the consequence system: the passive sponsor modifier that already follows your satisfaction live (±20% at the extremes, see 'What is board satisfaction?'). It used to only show as a raw '×1.10' number; now there's also a short sentence describing the effect in plain language, so it's clear this is already active before any of the harder consequence layers (2-6) kick in.

clubDnaFaq.q: What is club DNA and when do I pick it?
clubDnaFaq.a: Club DNA is your club's identity, one of 5 hand-crafted archetypes: Scandinavian development team, Italian classics traditionalist, Sprint-focused commercial, French climber heritage or British all-rounder. You pick it from the start of season 1, as soon as the board is open. The board analyses your team's national core and primary specialisation and suggests 3 options: one national match, one specialisation match and one wildcard. You can pick freely among the three. The DNA is final until the drift mechanic arrives (gradual evolution over 5 seasons, delivered in a follow-up slice).

clubDnaEffects.q: What does club DNA do exactly?
clubDnaEffects.a: DNA affects three things: (1) Your 5-year plans get an extra DNA tradition target (e.g. 'at least one Monument podium' for italian_classics or 'min. 2 stage jerseys/season' for sprint_commercial) as a bonus target on top of the focus-based ones. (2) Targets that match the DNA's priorities get boosted satisfaction bonus + penalty (italian_classics × 1.6 on monument_podium targets, if you win, the board cheers extra; if you miss, the penalty is larger). (3) When the chairman is replaced (after 2× plan expiry below 30% satisfaction), DNA bonus tips the alignment score towards archetypes that match the club's identity (italian_classics gives +4 to classics_purist and -2 to gc_lover, so the chairman reflects the DNA over time).

midSeasonCheckFaq.q: What is the mid-season check, and why am I getting an 'Action required' notification mid-season?
midSeasonCheckFaq.a: When half of the season's race days are over, the board checks your plan status. If satisfaction is under 50% OR at least half of your plan targets are 'behind' after half-time → you get a 'Mid-season check' notification in the inbox 'Action required'. The notification is informational: the board is worried but doesn't act automatically. You can respond by sending a board request (pivot plan targets), requesting a budget loan on the Economy page, or simply sharpening focus for the rest of the season. The banner only fires once per season per board.

majorPivotCooldown.q: What is pivot cooldown and a 'major pivot'?
majorPivotCooldown.a: A major pivot is a focus shift between extremes: from youth development to star signing or vice versa. These are allowed only once per plan lifecycle. When you e.g. accept 'more stars now' from a youth track, the board records that you've used your major pivot for this plan, and the next similar request is rejected with 'The board has already accepted a major pivot'. It resets when the plan is renewed (fresh plan = fresh cooldown). Pivots to/from balanced are not major: you can still pivot gradually via a balanced intermediate step.

endOfSeasonBlock.q: Why can't I send board requests in the season end phase?
endOfSeasonBlock.a: In the last 5 race days of a season, all requests are blocked. The board doesn't want to risk the plan being pivoted right before the evaluation, that would lose the link between your choices and the outcome. When there are fewer than 6 race days remaining, you see a 'The season end phase has begun' message on all request buttons. The window opens again at season start. Use the phase to score the last points instead.

multiYearPlanLock.q: Why can't I pivot my 5-year plan before it's halfway done?
multiYearPlanLock.a: 5-year and 3-year plans are locked for the first half of their runtime. Specifically: the board doesn't accept requests on a multi-year plan before at least 50% of the plan is completed OR your satisfaction has changed by more than 30 points since plan start. This prevents impulsive flip-flops on long-term plans and gives the board time to see whether the strategy is working. 1-year plans have no equivalent lock.

tightenedBadge.q: What does the 'Tightened' badge mean on a target in my new plan?
tightenedBadge.a: The badge shows when a target has been raised due to a tradeoff lock from a previous request. If you e.g. got 'Lower the result pressure' approved last season, your new plan's U25 or national target gets +1 in demand (or sponsor_growth +5pp under 'Ease the identity demand'). It's the deferred consequence of easing something: the board expects to see more of something else in return. The tightening goes away again after ONE season.

relativeRankLive.q: How does the 'finish ahead of X other managers' target work live?
relativeRankLive.a: On the Board page the balanced plan's relative_rank target now shows live status: 'You're #4 of 8 managers in the division, beating 4 (target: 3 ✓)'. The number updates after each race day when the standings refresh. It measures relatively to the number of human managers in your division, so it scales naturally from ~19 now to 100+ managers after open beta without cross-division noise.

season0To1Special.q: What happens at the transition from season 0 to season 1?
season0To1Special.a: Season 0 is the opening phase with no races, points or salaries. When season 1 starts you already hold your founding budget, so the system does not pay a season-1 sponsor on top (that would be double income at the start), and running costs (upkeep) are held back until the season is actually underway. From season 2 onwards the normal cycle runs at season start: 1) sponsor is paid out, 2) loan interest is deducted, 3) salaries are deducted, 4) upkeep is deducted, 5) an emergency loan is taken if there's still a shortfall. Season end only handles division bonus, promotion/relegation and board plan evaluation.

season1RaceCalendarFaq.a: Season 1's calendar is designed before the window closes and is published on the Races page (Season & Results → Races → Calendar). WorldTour races (Tour de France, Giro, Vuelta, monuments) are not run in season 1 by decision in the opening phase, so managers can find their footing in ProSeries and lower classes first. The full list shows up in the Races tab as soon as admin has approved the calendar.

howToWinRace.q: How do I win a race, and what decides the result?
howToWinRace.a: Results are decided by how well your riders' abilities fit the terrain. Every race is split into stages, and each stage has a terrain type (flat, hilly, mountain, time trial, cobbles and so on) that asks for specific abilities. A flat stage rewards Sprint above all; a mountain stage rewards Climbing; a time trial rewards Time trial; a cobbled stage rewards Cobbles; a hilly stage rewards Punch. For each rider the engine adds up the matching abilities, weighted by what the stage demands, into a stage score. On top of that score sit a few smaller effects: good form gives a small edge and high fatigue a small penalty (each capped at about ±3%, so a fresh rider beats a tired one of equal ability but a favourite still wins on class), a captain protected by strong, fresh helpers gets a boost, breakaways occasionally stay away on the right terrain, and a small random factor means the strongest rider wins most of the time but not always. Across a stage race, the time gaps from every stage add up into the overall classification: mountains and time trials open the biggest gaps and usually decide the GC, while flat stages finish in a bunch. Finishing positions become points, and points become both your leaderboard score and your prize money. The practical takeaway: match each rider to the stages that suit him, keep your stars rested for the days that matter, and set the right captain in Team selection. Each race also has a team classification, won by the team whose best three riders have the lowest combined time.

riderAbilities.q: What do the 15 rider abilities mean?
riderAbilities.a: Every rider is rated 1-99 on 15 abilities, grouped in three families. Physical (10): Climbing (long ascents and mountain stages), Time trial (riding alone against the clock), Flat (holding speed on flat roads as a rouleur), Tempo (sustained efforts on mid-mountain and rolling terrain), Sprint (top-end speed for a bunch finish), Acceleration (the explosive kick to launch a sprint or jump a gap), Punch (short steep climbs and hilly finales), Endurance (lasting the full distance of long and hard days), Recovery (bouncing back between stages and days), and Durability (resisting fatigue so the rider fades less late in hard races). Technical (3): Descending (a small edge on stages that finish on a downhill), Cobbles (the bike-handling and power for cobbled classics), and Positioning (staying near the front to avoid splits, and a factor in time trials). Tactical/mental (2): Aggression (the willingness to attack and get into breakaways) and Tactics (race-craft that helps in team time trials and reading a race). The race engine matches these to each stage's terrain, so the same rider can be a star on one profile and ordinary on another. Tip: a balanced squad covering several profiles scores more consistently across a calendar than a one-trick team.

riderRating.q: What does a rider's overall rating mean?
riderRating.a: The rating is a single 1-99 score for how good a rider is, shown as a coloured circle on the rider profile and as a sortable column in the rider rankings. It blends two things equally: how strong the rider is at their best type (climber, sprinter, GC and so on) and their all-round ability across all skills. It uses the same model that decides the rider's displayed type and market value, so the three always agree. The scale is anchored to the whole rider pool rather than to a fixed maximum, so the very best riders sit near 99 and the rest of the field spreads out below them. The rating reflects current ability only. It is not potential, and it does not include form, fatigue or how well the rider suits a specific race.

raceSignupFaq.q: Where do I sign up for a race?
raceSignupFaq.a: Go to Season & Results, Races (or open /races directly). The Calendar tab opens on your current race day by default, which can look empty or already in progress if there's nothing left to enter today. Use the day navigation (the Previous/Next day arrows) to step forward to an upcoming race day, then add riders to any race shown there. If you don't pick manually, your assistant auto-fills a lineup for you before the race starts.

raceClassificationsFaq.q: What's the difference between GC, Classic/Monument and stage wins?
raceClassificationsFaq.a: Every race has a class (such as ProSeries, Monuments or a Grand Tour) and a type: stage race or one-day race. Stage races (like Grand Tours) run several stages and track two separate results: a stage win for topping an individual stage, and a GC win for leading the overall classification once the race finishes. One-day races (Classics and Monuments) only ever produce a single result when they finish, so winning one always counts as a GC win, never a stage win, even though there's no multi-day overall classification to speak of. Your team stats and achievements count stage wins and GC wins separately.

newPlayerEconomyFaq.q: How much money do I start with, and when should I take a loan?
newPlayerEconomyFaq.a: You start with a {startingBalance} CZ$ budget in Division 3. Prize money from races, sponsor payouts at season start and rider sales all add income; salaries, upkeep and loan interest are deducted automatically at the start of each season. If your balance and sponsor income can't cover that season's salaries, the game takes an automatic emergency loan to cover the shortfall, so you're never blocked from paying wages. You can also take a loan yourself from the Finances page if you want to bid big on a rider before your income catches up. Each division has a debt ceiling (Division 3: 600,000 CZ$) that caps how much you can borrow at once.

academyIntakeScoutingFaq.q: How often do I get new Academy prospects, and does scouting reset?
academyIntakeScoutingFaq.a: Academy intake happens once per season: at the start of each season (or when you first set up your team) you get a small cohort of prospects to review and sign 0-2 of into your Academy. There's no extra cohort mid-season. Scouting works differently: you get 15 scouting actions per season, refilled automatically at the next season start, and you can spend them on any rider, not just your own Academy prospects, to narrow down how good they really are.

fatigueInjuryThresholdFaq.q: How high can I push fatigue before training risks an injury?
fatigueInjuryThresholdFaq.a: Injury risk from training only kicks in once a rider's Fatigue reaches 70 and you set Hard intensity; below 70, Hard training carries no injury risk at all. Above that, the risk grows with fatigue, so keep an eye on the risk badge on the rider before you save the program. This applies to Academy riders too: they train through the same daily program as your senior squad and can pick up an injury from Hard training, even though they can't be selected for races yet.

dayFormFaq.q: Why do race results vary from day to day?
dayFormFaq.a: Every rider gets a day form for each race day, so the same rider can fly one day and struggle the next. On rare days a rider cracks completely and loses big, and favorites are not immune. Riders in good form crack less often, and form also counts more in the result itself, so form management pays twice. Over a long stage race the good and bad days largely even out, but a single collapse on a mountain stage can decide a GC, just like in real cycling.

crashesFaq.a: During a stage a rider can crash or suffer a mechanical. Most incidents cost time on the result, but some force the rider to abandon the race: an abandoned rider is out of the remaining stages and all classifications, and is injured for a few days afterwards. Incidents are rare and hit hectic finales and cobbled races hardest, while time trials are the safest. Riders with good field positioning are hit less often. Abandons are listed on the race page.

whyReportFaq.q: How can I see why a result happened?
whyReportFaq.a: Race pages explain notable outcomes in plain language, not just numbers. Small badges next to a rider's name flag things like a helper who sacrificed his own placing for the captain, a pre-stage favorite who had an off day, or a rider who timed his form peak perfectly. A 'The bigger picture' block on the stage page adds a sentence for bigger swings, like a change of overall leader. The exact numbers behind a result stay hidden; only the story is shown.
